pages.text: Stress–strain relationship in axial compression for concrete using recycled saturated coarse aggregate González-Fonteboa Belén a,⇑, Martínez-Abella Fernando a,1, Carro López Diego b,2, Seara-Paz Sindy c,3 a School of Civil Engineering, Department of Construction Engineering of the University of A Coruña, ETSI Caminos, Canales, Puertos, Campus Elviña s/ n, 15071 La Coruña, Spain b School of Civil Engineering, Department of Construction Technology of the University of A Coruña, ETSI Caminos, Canales, Puertos, Campus Elviña s/n , 15071 La Coruña, Spain c School of Civil Engineering, University of A Coruña, ETSI Caminos, Canales, Puertos, Campus Elviña s/n, 15071 La Coruña, Spain article info Article history: Received 21 May 2010 Received in revised form 3 November 2010 Accepted 14 November 2010 Available online 14 December 2010 Keywords: Recycled concrete Stress–strain curve abstract In this work the stress–strain curve of the recycled concretes was determined by replacing different per- centages of the natural coarse aggregate with recycled coarse aggregate (20%, 50% and 100%). The results made it possible to establish the differences between the stress–strain relationship of a conventional con- crete and this relationship for a recycled concrete depending on the percentage of replacement. In this way, it was found that the longitudinal strain of the recycled concretes increases with the percentage of recycled coarse aggregate used. Finally, using the experimental results, an analytical expression of the stress–strain curve accounting for the percentage of replacement was developed. The veriﬁcation of the proposed model equation was done by comparing it to the experimental data. The results show that the proposed model equation satisfactorily describes the effect of the recycled aggregate on the stress–strain curve. Published by Elsevier Ltd. 1. Introduction The production of concrete with recycled aggregates from waste structural concrete is still rare in Spain. The new Spanish standard EHE08 [1] contains an appendix which presents several guaranteed recommendations intended to introduce the replacement of natu- ral coarse aggregate with recycled coarse aggregate up to 20%. For higher percentages, the effect of this replacement on the different physical, mechanical or chemical properties may vary. In fact, one of the main causes of the differences between recycled concrete and conventional concrete is the presence of adhered mortar to the recycled aggregates. Due to the adhered mortar (whose modulus of elasticity is low), the modulus of elasticity of recycled concrete is lower than that of control concrete with natural aggregates. Most authors [2,3] detect greater decreases than those observed in compressive strength, and they were found to be more pronounced as the replacement rate increased. In addition, for recycled concretes, speciﬁc studies [4–7] present experimental stress–strain curves which are substantially different from those of conventional concretes. Most of these studies do not, however, provide ﬁnal conclusions. Thus the structural design of these concretes under ultimate limit state according to the theories used for the conventional concretes is permitted. So, this paper at- tempts to address the lack of knowledge that has been detected in this ﬁeld. 2. Objectives For the non-linear analysis of structural members of concrete, it is necessary to determine their real stress–strain curves in com- pression. The curves propos

functions. The equation in Eurocode 2 [31] for the ascending and the descending branch up to a strain level of 3.5 ‰ and up to a con- crete strength level of 50 MPa is deﬁned as a continuous function. 3. Experimental program The design consisted of two different conventional concretes; one with a water- to-cement (w/c) ratio of 0.65, and one with a w/c ratio of 0.50. Next, recycled con- cretes using 20%, 50% and 100% of recycled coarse aggregate were designed and eight types of concretes were obtained, H-0.65-0, H-0.65-20, H-0.65-50 H-0.65- 100, H-0.50-0, H-0.50-20, H-0.50-50 and H-0.50-100. Then, tests were carried out to determine their basic properties: consistency, density, compressive strength using cubic and cylindrical specimens, tensile strength, longitudinal modulus of elasticity and Poisson’s ratio. Finally, tests were performed on each of the eight concretes to obtain the stress–strain relationships. Two types of tests were carried out: axial compression tests under controlled load rate according to the standard and axial compression tests under carefully controlled strain rate to obtain the descending branch beyond the peak stress [10]. In every test the load was measured. Two measurements of ax- ial strain and another two measurements of transverse strain were recorded. These experimental results allow us to deﬁne the ascending and descending branch of the stress–strain curve of each concrete. 3.1. Materials The materials used in this research were the following: Cement (C) : Portland CEM I – 42,5 N/SR cement. Water-reducing admixture : in order to obtain suitable workability, superplasti- cizer SIKAMENT 500 HE (S) was used. Recycled aggregates (Fig. 1 ): material obtained from real demolition debris of structural concrete was used. This was made up principally of concrete and stone. The size fraction used was 4–16 mm (4–16R) and its ﬁneness modulus was 6.65. Natural aggregates (Fig. 1): two size fractions of crushed limestone coarse aggre- gates (CA) were used, one with nominal sizes of 4 mm and 12 mm (4–12N) and a ﬁneness modulus of 6.24 and the other with nominal sizes of 8 mm and 20 mm (8–20N) and a ﬁneness modulus of 7.30. Finally a size fraction of natural ﬁne aggre- gate (FA), with nominal sizes of 0 mm and 4 mm (0–4N) with a ﬁneness modulus of 3.05 was used. In Fig. 1, the grading curves for each aggregate are displayed, show- ing the similarities between the curves for recycled and natural coarse aggregate resulting from the mixture between fractions 4–12N and 8–20N (natural coarse aggregate mix), Table 1 summarizes the basic properties of the aggregates used. 3.2. Concrete mixtures The Faury method was used as the basis for the design of the concrete mixes. The amount of water-reducing admixture was adjusted taking the consistency into account. The water incorporated by the additive was considered in the total amount of water. Coarse aggregates were pre-wetted. In fact, the greatest distinctive features of recycled aggregates compared to nat- ural aggregates is their lower density and higher absorption capacity, due mainly to adhered mortar [11–15] . A high absorption capacity inﬂuences fresh concrete prop- erties, particularly its consistency. This has given way to a number of studies in which essentially two alternatives are presented: to work with dry aggregates while increasing the amount of water incorporated in the mix [3], or prewetting. The latter poses in turn several processing options: presoaking for 24

using cylindrical specimen ( fc,cyl) and using cubic specimen ( fc,cub), tensile splitting strength ( fct, UNE-EN 12390-6:2001), longitudinal and transverse modulus of elas- ticity (UNE 83316:1996), values of the secant modulus of elasticity between stress 0% and 40% of the maximum stress ( E c) and initial tangent modulus (slope at the origin, Ec,tg), density and water absorption (UNE-EN 12390-7:2009). Four strain gauges were ﬁtted over the specimens used in the longitudinal and transverse modulus of elasticity tests and in the compressive strength tests; two were used to measure axial strain and the other two were used to measure trans- verse strain. Thus, throughout these tests, loads and strains were measured, making it possible to deﬁne the stress–strain curves under controlled load rate (ﬁxed at the standard value of 8.77 kN/s) up to the maximum stress and for the elastic branch. Next, compression tests on cylindrical specimens using controlled strain rate (ﬁxed 16 le/s) were developed. In the same way, loads and strains were measured throughout these tests. The controlled strain rate was achieved using displacement transducers and the strain measurements were taken using both the transducers and the gauges. Fig. 2 shows a specimen with the instrumentation. This control al- lowed the values of the ultimate strains in compression to be determined. 4. Tests results 4.1. Basic properties The results of the basic properties of the concretes are shown in Table 3 . The goal of this research is, as layed out in the introduction, to determine strain–stress behaviour of recycled concrete, stating the possible differences that may arise with common concrete with the same mixing proportions. To achieve this goal, two mixes were de- signed using the Faury method, based on the previous selection of w/c ratio and cement content. The same method was used on trial batches, keeping w/c ratio and cement content constant. Concrete was mixed with the aggregate prewetting method described above. The results of the basic properties of the concretes are shown in Table 3 . the ascending and the descending branch of the stress – strain curves of the recycled concretes, and conventional concretes as well. /C15 In this way, the equations for the generic stress–strain curves used in the Eurocode [31] can be used when designing recycled concrete structures, correcting the following parameters: Ecm, ec1 and ecu1. Acknowledgments This study is part of the Project entitled ‘‘3.2-358/2005/3-B: an experimental study on the use of RCDs in recycled concrete used in structural applications (RECNHOR)’’, ﬁnanced by the Spanish Envi- ronmental Ministry. In order to continue with this line of research a new Project has been assigned to the group. It belongs to the Pro- gram CENIT (Spanish Ministry of Industry) under the name ‘‘Clean, efﬁcient and nice construction along its life cycle (CLEAM)’’. References [1] EHE08. Recycled concrete consistency is inﬂuenced by aggregate absorption capacity. Thus, as explained in Section 3.2, some authors recommend presoaking or prewetting of aggregates. In this work, using pre-wetted coarse aggregates and a constant additive rate, slump values were constant for the different types of con- cretes, regardless of the percentage of replacement with recycled aggregate. The consistencies measured using slump values re- mained in the range of ﬂuid consistency (10–15 cm slump) for H-0.65 concretes or liquid consistency (over 15 cm slump) for H-0.50 concretes. The adhered mortar of the recycled aggregates causes a de- crease in the density values of recycled concrete and an increase in their absorption capacity [11,21]. The use of recycled aggregates, which have greater water absorption capacity than natural aggregates, made the water absorption values obtained with the recycled concretes higher than those of conventional ones. Moreover, the greater the per- centage of replacement, the higher the water absorption values will be. According to Katz [24], the decrease in the density values comes from lower bulk-speciﬁc density of recycled coarse aggregates whose presence also generates an increase in air content. Absorption capacity of recycled aggregates and the moisture state in which they are used inﬂuences both fresh concrete and hardened concrete properties. When presoaked or pre-wetted aggregates are used, some authors conclude that there is no signif- icant difference between the strength of conventional concretes and recycled concretes [21,13,19]. However, other authors con- clude that in the case of using the recycled aggregate in saturated state, the high water content inside the aggregate particles may re- sult in ‘‘bleeding’’ during casting; consequently, the compressive strength of the concrete would be reduced [17]. In this research, by working with recycled aggregates of rela- tively good quality and prewetting coarse aggregate through the process described in Section 3.2, compressive strength underwent only a slight decrease with the use of recycled aggregate. In partic- ular, for H-0.65 concrete, replacement percentages of 20% and 50% hardly show any differences with control mixes. Only concrete with 100% replacement shows a reduction of 5.6% compressive strength. With concrete type H-0.50, reductions of 2.4%, 16.4% and 9.5% are respectively shown for 20%, 50% and 100% replacement. Analyzing tensile splitting strength results, it can be observed that the incorporation of recycled aggregates has no signiﬁcant inﬂuence on this property, so the splitting tensile-to-compressive strength ratio of recycled concretes are similar to results obtained for conventional concretes made with natural aggregates, which agrees with conclusions reported by other authors [21,25–27] . However, the values of the longitudinal and transverse modulus of elasticity were affected by the use of recycled aggregate, since considerable decreases with the percentage of replacement have been detected. These decreases are caused by the used of recycled aggregate with a lower Young modulus than conventional aggre- gate. Various authors [28,29] show greater decreases in modulus of elasticity than those obtained for compressive strength, and more pronounced as replacement percentage is increased, how- ever, there exists a fairly degree of diversity in the results [30].I n this case, for H-0.65 concrete, decreases in the value of the modu- lus o

descending branch at a given value of the maximum stress (in this case 0.6 of the maximum stress) and the shape of the ascending and descending branch, which is dependent on the compressive strength of the concrete. Figs. 3 and 4 show, for different percentages of replacement, the stress–strain curves using controlled load rate and controlled strain rate for H-0.65 and H-0.50 concretes respectively. Longitudinal modulus of elasticity : as previously described in this study, the secant modulus of elasticity decreases when the use of recycled aggregate replacement increases. Ascending branch and peak strain : with regard to the shape of the ascending branch of the stress–strain curve, observing Figs. 3 and 4 it can be concluded that all the concretes present very similar stress–strain curves regardless of the recycled aggregate replace- ment ratio. The shape is linear up to 0.4 fc, and then it becomes par- abolic. However, a considerable shift of the curves was observed. In this way, with the same unit stress, the recycled concretes develop strains which are higher than the strains achieved with the con- ventional concretes, and the higher the replacement percentage is, the higher they will be. The presence of mortar adhered to the recycled aggregate, which is more compliant than the conventional aggregate, allows the increase of strains in the ascending branch. Consequently this increase leads to an increase in the peak strain value which is particularly signiﬁcant when the recycled aggregate is used with a replacement ratio of 100%. These results agree with those obtain for Xiao [5] and Li et al. [6]. Descending branch and ultimate strain : the shape of the descend- ing branch is also similar for concretes with similar compressive strengths ( Figs. 3 and 4 ). Also a decrease in the slope is detected when the percentage of recycled aggregate is increased. Hence, this decrease implies an increase in the ultimate strain (strain at which failure is deﬁned). As in the case of the peak strain, this increase is especially signiﬁcant when the percentage of replacement of con- ventional coarse aggregate with recycled coarse aggregate reaches 100%. Again, the presence of mortar adhered to the recycled aggre- gate, which is more compliant than the conventional aggregate, ac- counts for the increase of strains in the descending branch. However, these results do not agree with those obtain for Xiao [5] and Li et al. [6]. They indicate that the slope of the descending branch of the recycled concrete curves decreases as the recycled aggregate concrete content increases which in turn indicates that the recycled aggregate concrete behaves in a more brittle manner than conventional concrete. Nevertheless, this fact does not show up either in the curves published by Rühl and Atkinson [4]. 4.3. Peak and ultimate strain The values of the peak strain (at the maximum stress) and the ultimate strain (strain at which failure is deﬁned) have been iden- tiﬁed for the different concretes. The calculations were performed using the controlled load rate tests and the controlled strain rate tests. In the latter, mean values were obtained using strain gauges and displacement transducers. The results are shown in Table 4 and Fig. 5 . It can be seen that the use of recycled aggregate increases the values of both peak and ultimate strain. This increase, when recy- cled concrete with 100% of recycled coarse aggregates is analysed, reaches values of 20.5% and 22.0% for peak

most widely used equations, was employed as the basic model equation. This expression, for the ascending branch and for the descending branch up to stress level 3.5 ‰ (ecu1) strain, is: rc=fcm ¼ð k /C1 g /C0 g2Þ=ð1 þð k /C0 2Þg; where g ¼ ec=ec1 and k ¼ 1:05 /C1 Ecm /C1j ec1j=fcm The current codes consider the values of the secant modulus of elasticity dependent on compressive strength. Speciﬁcally, the Eurocode [31] provides the following expression for the determi- nation of this modulus, Ecm =2 2/C1 (fcm/10)0,3. In the same way, the Eurocode [31] provides the following expression for the determi- nation of the peak strain ec1 = 0.7/C1 (fcm)0,31. According to the results obtained in this study, the modiﬁcation of the stress–strain curves would imply that the code equations used for conventional concretes can be used for recycled concretes establishing three transformation coefﬁcients: one for the secant modulus Ecm (urec cmÞ; another for the peak strain, ec1 and ec2 (arec c ) and the last one corresponding to the ultimate strain, ecu1 and ecu2 (brec cu ). These coefﬁcients were adjusted from a regression analyses of the experimental results obtain with H-0.65 and H-0.50 ( Fig. 6 ). The following expressions, dependent on the percentage of replacement with recycled coarse aggregate (%RCA), were obtained for the coefﬁcients for modulus Ecm, for the peak strain and for the ultimate strain: urec cm ¼/C0 0:0020 /C2 %RCA þ 1 arec c ¼ 0:0021 /C2 %RCA þ 1 brec cu ¼ 0:0022 /C2 %RCA þ 1 5.2. Comparison of the proposed model equation and the experimental results of this study In order to verify the suitability of the proposed modiﬁcation, comparisons were drawn between the proposed model equation and the experimental results of this study. Figs. 7–10 show the experimental results, the code curve and the ﬁtted curve for con- crete H-0.65. When the ascending branch is analysed, a good adjustment between the experimental ascending branch of the curve and the ﬁtted curve is found. There is, however, quite a dif- ference when the descending branch is studied. Based on the frac- ture properties of the concrete, cracks do no occur in a stable and regular manner. As a result the descending branch of the stress– strain curve is expected to be unstable. However, an accurate adjustment between the experimental descending branch of the 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 3.5 4.0 4.5 5.0 0 20 40 60 80 100 % of replacement Strain (%o) ε peak H-0.65 load control ε peak H-0.50 load control ε peak H-0.65 strain control ε peak H-0.50 strain control ε ultimate at 0.6fc H-0.65 strain control ε ultimate at 0.6fc H-0.50 strain control Fig. 5. curve and the ﬁtted curve was found. In both cases, the ﬁtted curve adjusts better to the experimental results than the code curve. 6. Conclusions In this study the effect of the use of recycled coarse aggregate under water saturated conditions on the stress–strain relationships of the recycled concretes was investigated and the following con- clusions can be drawn: /C15 The shape of the stress–strain curves of the recycled concretes is similar to that of conventional concretes. However, a shift to the right in the stress–strain curves of the recycled concretes was detected. This may be attributed to differences in the fol- lowing parameters: the longitudinal modulus of elasticity and peak and ultimate strain. These displacements are of consider- able importance when the percentage of replacement is high. /C15 The longitudinal strain of the recycled concretes increases with the percentage of recycled coarse aggregate used, so that the slope of the stress–strain curves decreases. This means that the values of modulus Ecm decrease. It also means that the strains, particularly the peak and ultimate strain, increase. When the recycled concrete with 100% recycled coarse aggre- gate was studied, mean decreases of 23.6% for the modulus and mean increases of 20.5% and 22.0% for the peak and ulti- mate strain, respectively were found. /C15 Using the experimental results and the existing model equation (speciﬁcally the Eurocode equation [31]), reasonable coefﬁ- cients for Ecm (urec cmÞ; and ec1 (arec c ) and ecu1 (brec cu ) to consider the effect of the use of recycled aggregate were proposed.
